chunk_summary: This section discusses entitlement-based nutrient gap estimation using intake fraction to evaluate pediatric nutrition programs and identify barriers to effective nutrient delivery.
learning_objectives: Understand the concept of intake fraction and its application in estimating nutrient gaps in pediatric nutrition programs.
Learn how to calculate nutrient gaps based on entitlement and consumption data for schemes like ICDS and PM POSHAN.
Identify proximal barriers to nutrient intake, such as taste, stigma, or illness-related anorexia.
Explore corrective actions like intake audits and meal-day tracking to improve program outcomes.
key_facts: ICDS THR entitlement for children aged 6–35 months: 500 kcal and 12–15 g protein per day.
PM POSHAN lower primary entitlement: 450 kcal and 12 g protein per school day.
Example calculation: If a child consumes 50% of ICDS THR entitlement, they receive ~250 kcal and 6–7.5 g protein, with an equivalent gap.
Plate-waste observation for PM POSHAN suggests ~70% consumption, resulting in ~315 kcal and 8.4 g protein per meal-day, missing ~135 kcal and 3.6 g protein daily.
Missed service days significantly impact scheme contribution; e.g., 10 meal-days/month halves expected nutrient delivery.
reference_quote: National Food Security Act, 2013 Schedule II Nutritional Standards: 'Entitlement-based nutritional standards are defined for children, pregnant women, and lactating mothers under public schemes.'
document_key_topics: Intake fraction methodology for pediatric nutrition programs
Barriers to effective nutrient delivery in public schemes
Corrective actions for improving program outcomes
